data.title: Netskope Begins IPO Effort On Growing Revenue, High Losses
data.description:
Netskope Is Growing Amid High Operating Losses

Netskope, Inc. (NTSK) has filed to raise $100 million in an IPO of its Class A common stock, according to SEC S-1 registration information.

NTSK provides data cybersecurity software and related services worldwide.

The company is growing revenue quickly and retention metrics are solid, but operating losses, while dropping, remain very high.

I’ll provide a final opinion when we learn more IPO details from management.

What Does Netskope Do?

Santa Clara, California-based Netskope, Inc. was founded to develop its SaaS, cloud-based Netskope One integrated security platform to assist organizations in securing their data assets.

Management is led by co-founder, Chairman and CEO Mr. Sanjay Beri, who has been with the firm since its inception in 2012 and was previously in various executive positions at Juniper Networks and was a co-founder of Ingrian Networks, a data protection and privacy solutions company.

The company's primary offerings and capabilities include:

  • Monitoring

  • Response

  • Compliance

  • 120 Edge Data Centers

Netskope has booked fair market value investment of $1.5 billion as of July 31, 2025 from investors, including Lightspeed Venture Partners, ICONIQ and Accel.

Sales and Marketing expenses as a percentage of total revenue have trended lower as revenues have increased, as the figures below indicate:

The Rule of 40 is a software industry metric that says that if the combined revenue growth rate and operating margin equal or exceed 40%, the company is producing an acceptable growth/operating margin trajectory. (Operating margin strips out non-cash charges like SBC.)

NTSK’s most recent calculation was only 3% as of July 31, 2025, so the firm was in need of significant improvement in this regard, per the table below:

(Source - SEC)

The firm’s dollar-based net revenue retention rate as of July 31, 2025 was 118%, a solid result.

What Is Netskope’s Market?

According to a 2023 market research report by Grand View Research, the global data-centric security market was an estimated $4.3 billion in 2022 and is expected to exceed $24 billion by 2030.

This represents a projected CAGR of 24.2% from 2023 to 2030, a very high growth rate.

The primary factors driving this expected growth are a continued transition to complex cloud environments and growing threats from various actors, especially entering the generative AI era, which may serve to multiply those threat vectors.

Also, the chart below shows the historical and projected future growth path of the US data-centric security market by solution type, through 2030:

Major competitive vendors or industry players include the following firms:

  • Egnyte

  • Forcepoint

  • Palo Alto Networks (PANW)

  • ZScaler (ZS)

  • Fortinet (FTNT)

  • Broadcom (AVGO)

  • Cisco (CSCO)

  • IBM (IBM)

  • Informatica (CRM)

  • McAfee

  • Micro Focus

  • Microsoft (MSFT)

  • NetApp (NTAP)

  • Orange Cyberdefense

  • Symantec (now NortonLifeLock)

  • Thales eSecurity (OTCPK:THLEF)

  • Varonis (VRNS)

The company sells its platform through channel partners, technology resellers and via its own in-house direct sales force.

The firm derives most of its revenue through the indirect channel and the pie charts below show revenue contribution by channel and region:

I’ve prepared a thumbnail SWOT analysis for Netskope:

Strengths

  • Integrated cloud-native security platform, 120 edge data centers, strong Fortune 100 customer base

Weaknesses

  • Heavy dependence on partner ecosystem for sales, high R&D requirement for evolving threat environment

Opportunities

  • Expanding its customer base to include US Federal Govt and mid-market, and grow into adjacent markets via M&A

Threats

  • Intense competition from larger and more entrenched players, reliance on third-party vendors, changing government regulations

Netskope’s Recent Financial Results

The firm’s recent financial performance is summarized as shown below:

As of July 31, 2025, Netskope had $261.4 million in cash and $1.4 billion in total liabilities.

The company has generated free cash flow for the twelve months ended July 31, 2025, of negative ($24.7 million).

Netskope’s IPO Plan

NTSK intends to sell shares of Class A common stock in an IPO that may reach $500 million in final size.

No existing or possibly new shareholders have indicated an interest in acquiring additional shares, at least as of the initial prospectus

Class A stockholders will receive one vote per share.

Class B shareholders will be entitled to twenty (20) votes per share.

Class C shareholders will have no voting rights.

Company, shareholder and executive lock-ups will be for the usual 180-day period from the effective date of the prospectus.

According to Netskope’s most recent regulatory filing, it plans to use the net proceeds as follows:

We intend to use the net proceeds from this offering for general corporate purposes, including working capital, operating expenses and capital expenditures. We also intend to use a portion of the net proceeds we receive from this offering to satisfy the anticipated tax withholding and remittance obligations related to the settlement of our outstanding RSUs in connection with this offering. We may also use a portion of the net proceeds to acquire or invest in complementary businesses, products, services, technologies, or other assets. We do not, however, have agreements or commitments to enter into any acquisitions or investments at this time.

(Source - SEC)

The firm’s presentation of the company roadshow is not yet available for viewing.

Pertaining to potential legal exposure, leadership said the company is not subject to legal claims that would have a material adverse effect on its operations or financial condition.

Listed underwriters of the IPO are Morgan Stanley, J.P. Morgan, BMO Capital Markets, TD Cowen, Citizens Capital Markets, Mizuho, Wells Fargo Securities, Deutsche Bank Securities, Oppenheimer & Co., BTIG, KeyBanc Capital Markets, Piper Sandler, William Blair, Santander and Credit Agricole CIB.

Netskop Is Growing Amid Still-High Operating Losses

Netskope seeks to go public to support its growth plans and working capital needs.

The company’s financials have produced fast-growing topline revenue, higher gross profit and gross margin, lower but still high operating losses and a recent swing to cash flow from operations.

Free cash flow for the twelve months ended July 31, 2025, was negative ($24.7 million).

Sales and Marketing expenses as a percentage of total revenue have trended lower as revenue has grown and its Sales and Marketing efficiency multiple was flat at 0.5x in the most recent reporting period.

The firm currently plans to pay no dividends and intends to keep any future earnings for its growth and working capital requirements.

NTSK’s recent capital spending history shows it has spent heavily on capital expenditures as a percentage of its operating cash flow.

The company’s Rule of 40 results have been in need of improvement, with a reasonably good growth rate for topline revenue largely offset by a significant negative operating margin.

The firm’s growth strategy includes expanding its penetration of existing customers, targeting the US public sector, growing its international presence and the potential for product expansion through M&A.

The market opportunity for providing data security is large and expected to grow at a high rate of growth in the coming years, so the company has strong industry growth tailwinds in its favor, but also significant competition.

Risks to the company’s outlook as a public firm include its ‘emerging growth company’ status, which allows management to produce less information for shareholders.

The company relies on a limited number of channel partners, of which the top five partners and their affiliates represented 35% of the firm’s revenue for 1H 2025.

When we learn management’s proposed pricing and valuation expectations for the IPO, I’ll provide a final opinion.

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be announced

data.title: AppLovin: Don't Chase Till The Bubble Implodes
data.description:
AppLovin: Justifying The Next Growth Engine Beyond Gaming

I've got to say that investors of AppLovin Corporation (NASDAQ:APP) have fought a sturdy and valiant battle in helping the stock defy gravity, while still charting a way back toward its 2025 all-time highs. Yet, with AppLovin's tech sector peers (XLK) notching a new zenith in August even as the stock remains more than 15% below its early 2025 peak, I think my caution on APP in my previous update remains apt.

As a reminder, I urged investors not to get carried away with the early gains outside its core gaming business. While the scalability and portability of its Axon-driven ad tech business model could spur new growth vectors in e-commerce and CTV, the momentum is still too nascent for us to justify according a steep premium on these opportunities.

Following AppLovin's Q2 earnings scorecard, I'm increasingly convinced that unless the ad tech software leader could ramp up its exposure in e-commerce (about 10% of overall revenue), we must express caution into buying too quickly into the optimism on its profitability growth inflection to a higher base.

Note that following the divestment of its less profitable Apps business, margins for its overall business are expected to stabilize above the 80% mark over the next 12 months. Management is also clear with the underlying profitability growth accretion and guidance, prognosticating incremental margins >80%.

However, I don't think the market is dumb, right? The divestiture isn't a new development, even though it was formalized only very recently (closed on June 30). Therefore, while the "new" metrics were reflected on AppLovin's P&L only recently, the market has already priced a new norm moving ahead. In other words, if the incremental growth momentum in its gaming or new verticals don't perform better than expected, wouldn't it be challenging to move the needle closer to the 85% mark? We are talking about very high margins here, which could also elevate execution risks substantially.

AppLovin: A Ceiling On Already High Free Cash Flow Margins?

Nevertheless, management still expects robust high free cash flow margins, with conversion maintaining at above the 60% mark. Analysts are increasingly positive on the operating leverage gains, although it's also clear that incremental growth could moderate over the next two years to above the 30% mark.

Hence, with FCF margins already at such a high level (Vs. sector median of 11%), isn't it reasonable for us to postulate that unless ongoing gains could ignite a new breakthrough level, justifying APP's high growth premium might prove to be more challenging than expected?

Still, credit must be accorded to management for keeping optimism high and alive at AppLovin's Q2 earnings call. If you have been following APP's developments over the past year, I believe you are keenly aware of advertiser momentum spurred by the company's AXON AI automation in performance advertising.

And, although the e-commerce vertical comprises about 10% of its current base, the company enunciated that it has met with a growing backlog of advertisers with an annualized revenue run rate of $1B and above that are keen to be onboarded onto AppLovin's platform. Hence, the company is working to capitalize on its robust growth momentum by working on a referral program that's slated to be launched later in early Q4, plausibly across most, if not all markets. AppLovin is clearly keen on playing on the bigger stage (which is necessary), moving past the smaller SMB base that mainly accounted for its core gaming anchor.

While I acknowledge that APP appears to be making solid headway into what's arguably a significantly larger growth vertical (e-commerce and CTV subsequently), the economics remain nascent, even if not unproven for the company.

AppLovin is confident that it could adopt a similar playbook predicated on its AXON-driven flywheel, allowing it to scale to a higher level in e-commerce advertising. Management has also provided clarity into its 2026 marketing playbook, with plans to leverage cross-platform ads in Meta (META), TikTok, and LinkedIn (MSFT). Hence, I think the gamut of growth optionalities parlaying into what's a highly significant TAM as compared to AppLovin's 2025 forecasted revenue of less than $5.6B shouldn't be downplayed (but also not overplayed).

Buy Or Sell APP Stock?

And yet, the common refrain that I must insist. It's not banal, trust me. Experienced investors who have "been there, done that" would remind us that valuations often don't matter, until they do. And when you hear of investors telling us that "this time, it's different," you might want to be really judicious.

What about APP's valuation? Clearly, unless you have been living deep inside a cave, I don't need to remind you that the stock remains richly valued. With a forward EBITDA multiple of 30x, it's close to the level that preceded a pullback in early June. Nevertheless, some optimistic investors might contend that the stock remains below the peak multiple of almost 42x struck early this year.

Sure, if you are a fan of buying the peak. Investors who chased the early 2025 highs are still nursing losses, but that shouldn't have surprised anyone who has a keen sense of not overpaying for growth. As for APP, while I have little doubt that it has demonstrated incredible business momentum, the next phase in its development remains unsettled.

While management has afforded glimpses of its go-to-market and also underscored its confidence of being able to replicate the successful flywheel that underpinned its strategy in gaming, we shouldn't be too quick to overstate the early gains of its nascent forays.

And, with the stock clearly priced for perfection (well above the tech median of 14.9x), could we really insist that the market hasn't given enough optimism to APP's growth prospects?

While I'm not inclined to downgrade the stock further (as I've also not assessed clear bearish signals), I think an upgrade back to Buy appears somewhat too aggressive.

I urge you to give the company more time to validate its gameplan, even as the market appears to have already priced in optimism into a relatively successful 2026. Hence, unless you believe that APP could "easily" replicate what it achieved in gaming into the new growth verticals, it might really pay NOT to throw caution to the wind.

Rating: Maintain Hold.

data.title: Kuaishou: Becoming Positive On AI Initiatives And Capital Return (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I'm turning bullish on Kuaishou Technology (OTCPK:KUASF) (1024.HK). The stock is an "Artificial Intelligence" or "AI" play, and boasts a positive shareholder return outlook.

My prior September 19, 2024 article touched on both its near-term top-line prospects and its mid-term margin outlook.

KUASF's 2Q25 Bottomline Was A Positive Surprise Thanks To AI-Related Initiatives

The firm's recent quarterly financials were disclosed in the previous week.

Its normalized net income went up one-fifth YoY to CNY 5.6B in Apr-Jun '25. This was 11% better than the CNY 5,079M (source: S&P Capital IQ) consensus estimate.

KUASF registered comparatively inferior 4Q2024 and 1Q2025 earnings growth rates of +8% and +4%, respectively on year-on-year terms.

AI played a major role in the group's second quarter beat.

The company is a leading short-form video platform operator in Mainland China. Its "Online Marketing Services" or "OMS" segment contributed 58% of its topline last year. KUASF describes this business providing "performance-based marketing services" and the "display of marketing contents for an agreed period of time" in its annual report.

KUASF's 2Q2025 OMS revenues were +13% higher on YoY terms, which was an improvement compared to 1Q25's 8% rise.

The group has been utilizing AI to enhance its ad-tech, which it refers to as "Universal Auto X" or "UAX". This has made it easier for brands to place ads on the platform's short videos.

The proportion of OMS's advertising expenditures contributed by UAX expanded from 55% in 4Q24 and 60% in 1Q25 to 65% for the latest three-month period. The company's analyst briefing commentary indicated that OMS obtained a "higher lead conversion rate" by relying on "large language models."

KUASF generated 28% of its FY24 revenue from e-commerce, its other major division.

Its 2Q25 e-commerce "Gross Merchandise Value" or "GMV" increased 18% year-on-year. The 1Q2025 and 4Q2024 GMV grew relatively slower at at +15% and +14%, respectively.

In its results release, KUASF noted it recently introduced "OneRec, an end-to-end generative recommendation large model" for "recommending short videos" to lift "user-product matching efficiency." It's reasonable that e-commerce operations are performing better, when appropriate content is delivered to receptive consumers.

Kuaishou's EBIT-to-sales ratio widened +200bps (basis points) sequentially and +250bps YoY to 15% in the 2nd quarter this year. This was driven by a solid +13% topline increase and associated operating leverage effects.

In the next section, I draw attention to KUASF's investment merits.

Eyes On New GenAI Solution And Maiden Cash Distribution

There are certain categories of stocks that are likely to find favor with investors. Among them is businesses with exposure to the "Generative AI" or "GenAI" theme. Another one is those whose capital allocation policies are aligned with the interests of minority shareholders. Kuaishou ticks both boxes.

An Overview Of "Kling AI" Or "KAI"

KUASF has China's equivalent of Sora or Veo. It introduced its own AI video generation model to the market in the middle of '24, before coming up with an upgraded version "Kling AI 2.0" in May this year.

There are indicators suggesting that KAI is at the very early innings of growth.

KAI's sales jumped by +67% to CNY 0.25B in 2Q2025 on a sequential basis. This is less than 1% of its group-level topline for the same time frame.

JPMorgan (JPM) issued a report (not publicly accessible) on August 7, 2025 titled "Kuaishou Deep Dive". Kling's estimated "Total Addressable Market in ads production and professional content products" is $0.1T or CNY 700B as per JPM"s research.

Also, investments are usually a good leading indicator of future revenues. KUASF has raised its full-year FY25 "Kling AI-related CapEx" guidance by +100% according to its results call disclosures.

Separately, KUASF announced its first-ever dividend since its February 2021 Hong Kong public listing, in tandem with its quarterly earnings release.

The HK$0.46/share payment is equal to a generous two-fifths of its 2Q25 bottom line.

KUASF has categorized this as a "special dividend". The stock's forward dividend-to-price ratio could be a decent 2.5%, assuming that it subsequently initiates a formal cash distribution policy based on a 40% payout.

Also, it has an existing HK$16B buyback plan that is in effect between June '24 and mid-2027. This implies that around HKD 1.3B of money needs to be spent on repurchases every quarter to complete the program.

Kuaishou is ahead of its target, having allocated HK$3.3 billion to the purchase of its own shares in 1H25. It translates into an annualized buyback-to-market cap metric of about 2.0%.

KUASF's Q2 press release emphasizes that "in future, the Company will consider measures including share repurchase and dividend payment as appropriate to give back to our Shareholders."

There's a good chance that Kuaishou could offer a mid-single digit percent shareholder yield (2.5%+2.0%) in future. This assumes that its buybacks continues and it starts paying out recurring dividends.

You Should Consider These Bear-Case Scenarios

It's possible that individuals and corporates become less interested in AI-related offerings over time. Demand for KUASF's "Kling AI" solutions might drop in that scenario.

Fund managers could lose confidence in the listed company because of poor capital allocation. In the worst situation, it di-worsifies into unrelated ventures and distribute a smaller-than-expected amount of cash.

The Final Word

Kuaishou is now deserving of a Buy rating.

Its 2Q25 net profit surpassed expectations with the help of AI-related drivers, and the AI video generation model operations have great potential. The group is also turning more shareholder-friendly with its ongoing repurchases and maiden dividend.

KUASF's upside is +29% (18/14) assuming a target valuation at a fair 1x "PEG" or "Price-to-Earnings Growth". It currently trades at a forward P/E of 14 times. The firm's consensus FY24-28 earnings CAGR forecast is +18% as per S&P Capital IQ.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Kopin: Accretive Defense Pivot Creates Opportunity, But Risks Keep Me At Hold
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Kopin Corporation (NASDAQ:KOPN) is one of those names that’s easy to overlook, especially given the company’s microcap status over the last two or so decades. The company chased the consumer AR/VR dream for more than a few years and mostly came up short. Investors lost interest, and for good reason. But now, it looks like the story is shifting. Kopin is leaning hard into defense and aerospace, supplying mission-critical displays for programs that actually matter and can scale.

I believe that there are two main reasons why we should be taking note of this shift, and I’ll talk about them later.

For me, the setup is about balancing the upside story with the risks. If defense wins convert and the margins improve, there’s incredibly promising upside with the stock. For now, though, I’m sticking with a very positive hold because of the risks, and I’ll revisit my position if management delivers in the second half of FY25.

Company Overview

Kopin primarily builds microdisplays and optics, mainly for defense and aerospace customers. Think supplying displays for mission-critical applications like night vision systems, weapon sights, and pilot helmets.

The company’s product portfolio covers several display types: AMLCD, OLED, FLCoS, and MicroLED, each with different use cases. In my opinion, Kopin stands out because of its positioning in MicroLED, where it’s currently the only US-based supplier. That is incredibly important in the current geopolitical environment because defense programs increasingly require domestic, secure supply chains for sensitive technologies.

Today, defense is the clear anchor for Kopin’s revenue, and many of the programs it’s working with are tied to the U.S. Army and Air Force. They make up the bulk of its backlog. I should mention that the company also has some exposure to consumer and industrial customers, but those are much smaller pieces of its business these days. The company is also now working with Theon International, and this partnership gives it access to NATO and European markets, which unlocks a different level of earnings potential.

Q2 2025 Review

Revenue came in at $8.45 million, which is a 31% drop YoY, and most of the pressure is from delays in defense program procurement. Those delays pushed shipments out of Q2, but during the earnings call, management stressed that the picture is still looking pretty. Orders picked up late in the quarter, so Kopin has a positive book-to-bill and more confidence for H2 2025 to be much stronger.

Then we have product gross margin, which was nearly nonexistent. The cost of product made up 94% of product revenue, so the company is still having issues with low volume and underutilization. On the call, management was very optimistic when highlighting the progress on new optical inspection systems, and the expectation is that utilization will improve when the delayed orders start flowing through in Q3 and Q4.

The balance sheet still looks stretched. Kopin ended the quarter with about $24.3 million in cash and equivalents plus $2.4 million in marketable securities. Balance that against an operating cash outflow of $7.6 million in the first half. We must also consider the going-concern flag (litigation) in the 10-Q report, most of it because of the company’s losses and uncertainty around the lawsuit’s final verdict.

All together, Q2 wasn’t pretty. But management’s confidence about order flow improving in the back half, alongside the newly announced Theon deal, is a big part of why the stock narrative is still very positive.

Defense Is The Key To Kopin’s Future

IVAS and SBMC Are Present and Future Revenue Keys

When I look at Kopin today, the story rests squarely on its repositioning as a defense-first supplier. And for once, the contracts and numbers actually back up management’s narrative.

The IVAS NOW and Soldier Borne Mission Command (SBMC) programs are the most important pieces here. We don’t have exact figures here, but going off what Derrick Zierler outlines in his article on the company following interviews with CEO Michael Murray, Kopin can potentially make up to $60 million over the next five years with IVAS NOW if they win orders starting in 2025. That is not a small number when you consider Kopin just reported $8 million in revenue for Q2. With SBMC, which is a $22 billion program (also known as IVAS NEXT), what we (and Kopin) are waiting for is its expansion into Phase 2. When that happens, we should see Kopin’s role grow meaningfully because they’re the only US manufacturer of monochrome microLEDs, which are the foundation for the program.

If we go back to the Q2 call, management sounds very excited about this, and I think they’re right. Here’s what they said about both programs and how they affect Kopin’s future outlook:

We expect prime contractors to be selected soon for this technology upgrade to the existing IVAS platform, which we believe will continue until SBMC becomes available. Along with the prime contractor selections, we expect announcements and awards for critical technology acquisition areas where Kopin fits into to be announced shortly as well. Given the long-term nature of many of the existing programs and the contract wins so far for 2025, our current pipeline is very strong and growing. As a reminder, several of our programs have congressional budget demands through 2030 and several of the contracts we have are indefinite demand and indefinite quantity, or IDIQs, which allow for even greater revenue demands than we currently have on order.

What I want to highlight is the long-term nature of the projects Kopin is getting into. These are operations that will be active for years on end, and given that defense spending is rising across NATO countries and the US, the potential for scale and extreme revenue growth over the next few years represents huge upside if Kopin can execute against the demand.

Additionally, once a company is validated in such a niche area like defense optics, the switching costs for its customers are high, so it’s unlikely that it will suffer revenue contraction once contracts are fully locked in. We can see this with Kopin’s recent contract extension from Lockheed Martin, one of the largest defense contractors in the world. If the business continues to execute at this rate, I think that the focus on defense will reset its trajectory entirely.

The Theon Partnership Can Be Transformational

The other thing I want to talk about is how the new partnership with Theon International changes the game for Kopin, at least in terms of how it’s perceived and its position in the defense contracting industry outside the US. Theon is a well-established optics player in Europe, and this alliance gives Kopin both credibility and access to NATO and European defense programs. The EU’s defense spending has risen from $218 billion in 2021, before Russia invaded Ukraine, to around $326 billion in 2024. That’s a ~30% increase over the period, and countries like Italy plan to double their defense spending from current levels over the next 8 years. So, the timing really couldn’t be better.

I’d describe the deal as almost transformational because it diversifies Kopin beyond U.S. Army dependence and opens up a channel into a region that is actively expanding its defense budgets. Even a modest win here can be very material for a company of this size. As management said during the earnings call:

With Theon's expertise in night vision sensors and systems, coupled with Kopin's capabilities in all 4 types of microdisplays and optical assemblies, we collectively believe the combination will bring the most advanced technology to market sooner at a lower price and within sovereign content required to be successful together on much larger pursuits and contracts globally…

Importantly, our firms will be able to partner together and compete on much larger projects and contracts, higher up the value chain. Global defense contractors and integrators are looking for more than simple commodity products to plug into systems. They want application-specific solutions tailored to their individual needs and products.

Clearly, the synergy benefits that both companies can unlock with this partnership go beyond mere sales of defense products. Licensing will be key, especially for Kopin, and I’ll be watching to see how they work with Theon under the non-exclusive licensing model in place.

One more thing. Theon’s working relationship with Kopin is largely based on the latter’s expertise in application-specific solutions like DayVAS and DarkWAVE. Management believes that this is where the biggest opportunities lie because of Theon’s market exposure with night-vision goggles and associated systems/products. The idea here is that sales under the partnership will start coming in by Q4 2025, along with some R&D revenue.

Along with that, it’s clear that Theon will find Kopin’s MicroLED technology and its position as the only U.S.-based supplier right now to be a real asset, given the DoD’s focus on domestic, secure supply chains. As such, we should also expect some level of order increase in this area from the partnership. On the technology itself, MicroLED is brighter, more efficient, and more rugged than alternatives like OLED or LCD, and those features are crucial for soldier-worn and pilot systems. Kopin has already delivered products that outperform their competitors in both durability and power efficiency. So, it has a clear advantage in terms of its product base and manufacturing capabilities, and we can see how the company can be a strategic asset moving forward.

That’s why I also see an M&A angle here. The larger primes like L3Harris and fast-moving players like Anduril are actively looking to secure next-gen defense technologies, and Kopin is already working on those. I believe that the company’s combination of IP and program exposure could make it an attractive bolt-on in the next wave of defense-tech consolidation.

Of course, Kopin is still a sub-$5 stock with a fragile balance sheet, and Theon’s $15 million investment (with a convertible $7 million of that in preferred shares) definitely adds some much-needed liquidity and momentum to the former’s narrative. As such, I’d say that the combination of SBMC, Theon, and its MicroLED moat explains why investors are finally looking at this as more than just another speculative turnaround. For the first time in years, it definitely looks like the pieces are in place for something bigger than the false starts of the past decade.

Valuation & Market Sentiment

Kopin is still a fairly tiny company by many metrics, but the stock is already rerating as if the turnaround is well underway. Shares are up around 76% over the past year, outperforming the broader small-cap tech basket. We can’t really do much with earnings multiples here: those are at a nice premium compared to the sector medians, mostly because of the rally.

I’d argue that for a company that has so many tailwinds working in its favor for the first time in a long time, the optimism is fairly warranted, and I share it. However, one may argue that the current valuation is a bit hard to justify on today’s earnings power. Remember, this is a business that just posted $8.45 million in quarterly revenue with negative gross margins. But I expect those are short-term problems.

I also think that management is very confident that the stock will rise in response to more bullish sentiment over the next few quarters, which is why Theon’s $7 million in preferred shares can be converted to common stock at $3/share. Also, Kopin can force the conversion if the “stock trades at $4.50 or higher for a period of 10 trading days within a 30 consecutive trading day period as defined in the agreements.”

However, the bullish sentiment needs to be backed up by more contract wins and revenue recognition because the stock will be trading on backlog potential instead of trailing financials. That raises the stakes, so any delay in procurement or margin recovery may snap that premium quickly.

Risks That Still Matter

I think that the biggest swing factor to investor sentiment, not the fundamentals, is still the BlueRadios lawsuit. If Kopin loses, the damages are big enough that they could easily take out a major chunk of its cash balance, and the company may even have to raise some capital from public markets. Of course, management insists that they’re defending it vigorously, but that is a cloud that won’t clear until the case is resolved.

The second risk is liquidity. Kopin closed Q2 with about $24 million in cash and another $2.4 million in securities, but it burned more than $7 million in the first half of the year. That doesn’t leave a lot of breathing room if orders slip or margins don’t improve. We have some mitigating factors here, though, because the company is counting on more orders, defense contracts, and Theon’s contribution to help, but the cushion is thin.

Finally, execution. The SBMC and IVAS programs are the main catalysts for Kopin’s growth, the way I see it, and they give the company a legitimate pipeline. However, it’s common knowledge that defense orders are lumpy, so if there are any delays in procurement, that pushes revenue out, which is what we saw in Q2. Margins are also still very tight; the cost of product was 94% of product revenue in Q2. What needs to happen here is that Kopin proves that it can scale and actually turn backlog into earnings, but until then, I’ll be watching this as a point of fragility.

Takeaway

For me, Kopin has a story that makes sense for investors. SBMC, Theon, and MicroLED give it a real shot at scaling into a meaningful defense supplier. The contracts are credible, the technology has an edge, and I can’t rule out an M&A if the larger primes want domestic MicroLED access. But the business is still fragile, and the stock already reflects a lot of optimism.

As such, I’m staying on a hold. I like the setup and the defense pivot, but I want to see orders convert to revenue in H2 2025 and margins improve before calling it a Buy.

data.title: Barrick Mining: Turnaround Thesis Remains Intact
data.description:
All figures are in United States Dollars unless a C$ or A$ is in front of the figure. G/T = grams per tonne (of gold or silver). GEOs = gold-equivalent ounces. SEOs = silver-equivalent ounces. AISC refers to all-in sustaining costs. LOMP = life of mine plan. TPD = tonnes per day. UG = Underground. OP = open-pit.

Q2 Gold & Copper Production

Barrick Mining Corporation (NYSE:B) released its Q2 '25 results recently, reporting quarterly production of ~797,000 ounces, a 16% decline vs. the year-ago period. This has continued a steep downtrend in gold production for the company, but the most recent dip was obviously out of its control, with the Loulo-Gounkoto Mine (one of Barrick's largest) offline to start the year due to inability to reach an agreement with the State of Mali (and since moved under temporary provisional administration on June 16th). However, if we adjust for Loulo-Gounkoto to create for like-for-like results (Q2 '24: ~137,000 ounces), production was only down slightly from ~811,000 ounces to ~797,000 ounces, while copper production increased materially to ~59,000 ounces, a 37% increase year-over-year.

Quarterly Production by Mine

Digging into the results a little closer, Nevada Gold Mines (61.5% Barrick/38.5% Newmont) saw lower production year-over-year, with ~381,000 ounces attributable to Barrick, down from ~401,000 ounces in Q2'24. Lower production was related to a decline in output at Carlin (~170,000 ounces at $1,826/oz AISC vs. ~202,000 ounces at $1,805/oz), offset by higher production at its three other Nevada sites (Cortez: ~108,000 ounces at $1,774/oz AISC, Turquoise Ridge: ~76,000 ounces at $1,621/oz AISC, Phoenix: ~27,000 ounces at $1,376/oz AISC).

As for the lower production at the largest of the four sites, Carlin's production fell because of lower throughput at its autoclave/roasters, with lower autoclave tonnes processed related to an increase autoclave blend from Cortez. Production was also impacted by the planned shutdown of the Gold Quarry Roaster in early Q2, offset by higher grades processed overall (4.50 G/T of gold vs. 4.19 G/T of gold). That said, Barrick expects a much stronger H2'25 from its Carlin Complex in line with guidance and lower costs, with back-end weighted production vs. the ~315,000 ounces produced year-to-date (715,000 ounce guidance midpoint at Carlin).

As for its Cortez Complex, higher production benefited from 31% higher grades processed and increased tonnes through the roasters/autoclave, with a feed grade of 2.69 G/T of gold vs. 2.05 G/T of gold in the year-ago period. Unfortunately, AISC was impacted ($1,774/oz vs. $1,447/oz) by higher royalties and sustaining capital (~$45 million vs. ~$39 million), more than offsetting the increased ounces sold in the period. However, like Carlin, Barrick expects a much better H2'25 here as well, with just 200,000 ounces of a planned 445,000 ounces produced, with better costs expected in H2'25 as well.

Moving to Turquoise Ridge, production was ~76,000 ounces at $1,621/oz AISC, with higher production but at higher costs (Q2 '24: ~72,000 ounces at $1,505/oz). Production was impacted by lower throughput (~570,000 tonnes) that partially offset the sharp increase in grades (4.74 G/T of gold vs. 4.22 G/T of gold), with this largely because of the planned shut of its Sage autoclave in Q2 '25 vs. a shut in Q1 '24 of the prior year. On a positive note, mining is moving into higher-grade zones at TR-UG in H2 with increased UG tonnes and grades expected to support a strong second half (YTD: ~150,000 ounces at $1,513/oz AISC vs. 327,500 at $1,310/oz guide midpoint).

Moving to Pueblo Viejo (60%) in the Dominican Republic, production was ~95,000 ounces at $1,552/oz AISC, up from ~80,000 ounces at $1,433/oz AISC in the year-ago period. All-in sustaining costs were up year-over-year on the back of higher sustaining capital spend and royalties offset by higher ounces sold, and are trending well above full-year guidance at $1,605/oz YTD vs. guidance of $1,330/oz (midpoint). Production in Q2'25 benefited from higher throughput and recoveries, with throughput outperforming expectations in Q2'25, with recoveries to improve further with recovery improvement projects on deck in H2. Once fully ramped up in 2027, Pueblo Viejo should produce closer to 500,000 ounces (60% basis) for Barrick with its 14 MTPA Expansion at more competitive costs, representing over 25% growth from its 2025 guidance midpoint (390,000 ounces at $1,330/oz AISC).

As for its Kibali Mine in the DRC (45%), production was ~75,000 ounces at $1,273/oz AISC, down from ~82,000 ounces at $1,086/oz AISC in Q2'24. Production was impacted by lower throughput and grades (~946,000 tonnes at 2.73 G/T of gold), with AISC up because of higher royalties and fewer ounces sold. However, like the bulk of Barrick's portfolio, H2'25 is expected to be better with just ~138,000 ounces produced in H1'25 of a planned 315,000 ounces at $1,180/oz, with Q2'25 impacted by an unplanned shaft rope change out that resulted in fewer UG tonnes.

As for some of its smaller assets, production at North Mara was ~62,000 ounces at $1,292/oz AISC, up from ~54,000 ounces at $1,491/oz on the back of higher grades. Meanwhile, Veladero saw higher production of ~62,000 ounces at $1,295/oz AISC on higher throughput and marginally lower grades (0.68 vs. 0.69 G/T of gold). Elsewhere, Hemlo's production was down year-over-year at higher costs, with Tongon also seeing lower production at higher costs.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Tongon/Hemlo: The latter two assets have been labeled as non-core and could be sold this year into what has become a seller's market, evidenced by a lot of more marginal assets getting scooped up for reasonable prices, including Pan/Gold Rock recently at a price of ~$166 per M&I oz.

While not insanely priced for a producing asset, this deal for Minera Alamos is not cheap for an asset of this quality, especially relative to Integra, that scooped up Florida Canyon for a much easier to swallow price with far less dilution -~$44 million net of cash that was picked up in the deal held by Florida Canyon or a price of ~$51/oz on M&I resources, also in Nevada, and also on a producing asset.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

As for Barrick's copper production, it came in at ~59,000 tonnes (+37% year-over-year), with a far better quarter from Lumwana on both production and costs. In fact, Lumwana's production came in at ~44,000 tonnes at $2.79/lb AISC, a 76% increase in production and a dramatic improvement vs. $4.36/lb AISC in the year-ago period, with cash costs also down sharply to C$1.58/lb following a transition to an owner stripping model that has driven efficiences. Q2'25 production benefited from higher throughput (~7.08 million tonnes) at higher grades (0.69%) and much higher recoveries (92% vs. 85%), while AISC benefited from increased tonnes sold and lower sustaining capital spend ($78 million vs. $102 million) year-over-year.

Financial Results

While the production results and headline costs weren't a blowout by any means, Barrick clearly guided to a much stronger H2'25, and the mediocre H1'25 results were largely to be expected. Fortunately, the record gold price (XAUUSD:CUR) did what was in-line performance overall (combined with the loss of its best asset on a production/margin standpoint - Loulo-Gounkoto in Mali) with Barrick reporting revenue of ~$3.68 billion (+16% year-over-year), operating cash flow of ~$1.33 billion (+15% year-over-year) and free cash flow of ~$395 million (+16% year-over-year) despite a significant increase in total capex (Q2'25: ~$934 million vs. ~$819 million, including ~$439 million in growth capex).

Importantly, these solid financial results were despite what are roughly peak capex levels for this business (~$4.0 billion annual run rate during a period of growth spend at multiple projects - Pueblo Viejo, REN, Goldrush, Lumwana, Reko Diq) and despite:

  1. fewer ounces of gold sold than produced.
  2. no contribution from its cash cow, Loulo-Gounkoto.

Hence, while these were not blowout financial results and certainly were not records for the company, they were solid when put in context, and investors can expect much better in the second half of this year. Plus, when it comes to things Barrick can better control, unlike the less pleasing short-term fate of Loulo-Gounkoto, the company bought back ~21.2 million shares YTD at ~$19.40/share, offloaded a $1.0 billion dollar headache of a project in Donlin that allowed it to improve an already strong balance sheet.

In addition, while the hard work has been done by uncovering and starting to get a good feel for the structure of the world-class Fourmile orebody that lies just north of its Goldrush deposit, recent drilling into this deposit continues to delivering jaw-dropping results, including a 1,500+ gram-meter intercept of 24.7 meters at 62.23 G/T of gold.

In fact, these results are even more impressive than the results that Bre-X dreamed up at Busang, including fictional intercepts of 26 meters at 12.9 G/T of gold and 69.3 meters at 7.2 G/T of gold.

Costs & Margins

Looking at costs and margins, Barrick reported Q2-25 AISC of $1,684/oz, a 12% increase year-over-year, despite lower sustaining capital spend year-over-year. Costs were impacted by lower production, no low-cost ounces from Loulo-Gounkoto and this has left AISC sitting at $1,591/oz YTD, well above its guidance of $1,460/oz - $1,560/oz with a very strong H2'25 needed to get costs closer to the top end of guidance. Fortunately, the gold price more than made up for the higher costs, with a $3,295/oz average realized price translating to $1,611/oz AISC margins (48.9%), a massive improvement and 1,280 basis point increase year-over-year from $862/oz in Q2 '24.

As for its copper business, Barrick put a meaningful dent in costs here, reducing copper AISC to $2.90/lb from $3.67/lb on the back of its much stronger results from Lumwana. This translated to a near doubling of AISC margins to $1.46/lb vs. $0.86/lb despite a lower average realized copper price ($4.36/lb vs. $4.53/lb), and costs for its copper business are only going to improve with Lumwana Super Pit and the ultra low-cost Reko Diq Mine coming online in 2028. In fact, Reko Diq is a near bottom decile asset from a cost standpoint, with AISC estimated to come in below $1.30/oz over the life of mine.

Let's dig into recent developments:

Recent Developments

Capital Allocation

Starting with capital allocation, Barrick has spent ~$411 million of its total $1.0 billion buyback program, and we're likely to see further purchases when the Q3 '25 results are announced in November. This is helped by a strong balance sheet (~$70 million net cash post Donlin sale) and Barrick's view that is stock is still very undervalued, which is certainly a fair assessment if looking at the long-term trajectory of this business and its growth to ~7.0 million GEOs/year by the end of this decade.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

"So I think we're on track to, we are committed to that $1.0 billion buyback strategy where the year-to-date is at $411 million. And so that's the way we will manage it....

....But certainly, buybacks is something that we will increasingly focus on given the fact that the shares are very undervalued."

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Fourmile

Moving to Fourmile, Barrick has completed ~34,000 meters of drilling year-to-date and shared that it believes it has the potential to double the resource by year-end, with this already being one of the most impressive resources globally, sitting at ~1.4 million ounces at 11.76 G/T of gold (indicated) and ~6.4 million ounces of gold at 14.1 G/T of gold (inferred). However, this does not include resource upside to the north of the current drilling focus (area shown in black dashes), with Fourmile still open to the north and at depth and the current ~7.8 million resource contained to just the red area. Hence, I don't think it's unreasonable at all to believe that this could be a 20+ million ounce resource longer-term through further drilling, and that could certainly support an 8-10 million ounce reserve base and ~500,000 ounces over 16-20 years.

As for infill drilling to date, early hits in the program have been truly astounding, with ~19.5 meters at 36.54 G/T of gold, ~9.9 meters at 39.2 G/T of gold, and ~18.4 meters at 59.42 G/T of gold. The best hit, though, was north of the Goldrush property cut-off (to the south if shared Newmont/Barrick ground) with Barrick hitting ~24.7 meters at 62.23 G/T of gold. These highlights are not just above current resource grades, they are multiples of current resource grades, and it's quite possible that Barrick's belief in 14 G/T mined grades might come to fruition in the best years for this asset, making this the closest the sector has seen to a Fosterville since the Swan Zone was depleted.

The major difference, though, is that Fosterville had five great years, and Fourmile is looking like it could have 20 great years, and that's just within the mineralized footprint drilled out to date. As for these results, Barrick's CEO has compared this to Goldstrike, American Barrick's famous discovery that sent the stock in a parabolic uptrend in the 1980s with share-price performance tracking the exponential production growth from this high-grade find (Goldstrike).

As the chart below highlights, Goldstrike would result in impressive share performance for American Barrick, with it being an asset that would see a near ~30x increase in production from 1987 to 1992 (~40,000 ounces to ~1.11 million ounces of gold) from a world-class and ultra high-grade ore body with industry-leading grades, with highlight intercepts like ~164.6 meters at 14.1 G/T of gold (Meikle, 1989).

And while Fourmile at its Cortez Complex looks like it could be of similar quality and could end being a 20+ million ounce resource base with a few more years of drilling at industry-leading grades, the major difference is that American Barrick was a much smaller producer at the onset of its period of growth with smaller producing assets like Mercur, Camflo, Renabie, Valdez Creek, Pinson, and Holt-McDermott.

Today, Barrick obviously has a far more diversified portfolio, meaning that while Fourmile is a game-changer for the company with it owning arguably the best undeveloped gold asset in a Tier-1 ranked jurisdiction, I certainly wouldn't expect anywhere near similar performance for the stock given that this is an asset with the potential to increase gold production by ~12% on an attributable basis at ~50% lower costs (~370,000 attributable ounces/year at 61.5% post-vend in ownership) vs. Goldstrike which almost single-handedly grew American Barrick's production by 500% (as shown below) as it grew towards 1.0 million ounce scale.

That said, there are major advantages to Fourmile today vs. Goldstrike in the 1980s, including the fact that Barrick has over 20 years of experience with roaster/autoclave technology, it already has operating roasters (1994, 2000) and an autoclave at its Carlin Complex for processing. Hence, it is in a far better position today from an infrastructure/development standpoint and from an experience today.

And similar to Fosterville, it's hard to put a value on this asset when you are getting results this impressive, and while I think 20 million ounce resource potential captures a chunk of the upside, this is an asset that should have a further exploration upside premium attached to it, as assets of this caliber often massively outperform even lofty expectations in terms of endowment.

Putting it all together, there are a few major benefits here for Barrick. The first is that Fourmile could displace material at 2x higher grades at its Carlin processing facilities. As for scale, it has the potential to see peak production years of over 550,000 ounces at sub $850/oz AISC, assuming mine throughput of 1.4 MTPA - 1.5 MTPA, with peak years for grade closer to 14 G/T of gold.

At a ~$3,200/oz gold price (2030-2049) assuming first stope production in the second half of 2030, Fourmile's estimated NPV (5%, 100% basis) is nearly $7.0 billion based on ~8.8 million ounces recovered over 20 years (~440,000 ounces/year on average) with this being a 100% Barrick project unlike the rest of Nevada Gold Mines (61.5% / 38.5% in favor of Barrick). Hence, Fourmile alone is arguably worth over US$4.00/share on ~1.7 billion shares and it is not even remotely in Barrick's valuation today.

This is because Barrick trades at less than 7.5x FY2026 cash flow estimates even using a conservative $3,100/oz gold price assumption and less than 7x FY2026 cash flow estimates at a $3,400/oz gold price assumption, barely half the multiple of Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM) and a 25% lower multiple than Newmont. This is despite no Fourmile, no Reko Diq, and no Lumwana Super Pit, as well as no Loulo-Gounkoto (Mali), but where a possible resolution at some point would provide a welcome boost to NAV and cash flow.

These multiples are ex-growth and on near trough production levels and are not only a discount to where Barrick has traded historically, but also to its peer group. And while Barrick certainly deserves to trade at a discount to Agnico Eagle which has enjoyed near flawless execution and has over 90% of its NAV tied to Tier-1 ranked jurisdictions, I don't see any reason for Barrick to be trading at this steep of a discount to Newmont, especially with Barrick having a similar proportion of production from Tier-1 ranked jurisdictions today, a better growth profile, and a far better track record of per share growth over the past several years.

Potential Divestments

As for potential divestments, Barrick has labeled Hemlo, Tongon and Zalvidar as non-core assets which could bring in more cash, and I like that the company is accepting offers on these assets in what is the strongest gold market in over a decade, an ideal time to be shopping assets. The company also shared that it has significant ordinary and capital losses in Canada to shield taxes if it finds a buyer for its Hemlo Mine in Ontario.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

".....We've added 110 million ounces of gold equivalent ounces to reserves in Barrick in the last 6 years. And so we invest in our future. We've brought some significant new reserves in the form of Reko Diq, and we've converted big reserves in Pueblo Viejo and likewise in Lumwana.

And so it makes sense to rationalize your portfolio from time-to-time. We did that immediately out of the blocks in 2019. As you recall, it's now quite a few years on. We've got growth ahead of us. It makes good sense to clean up the portfolio. And it's a good time to do it when there are buyers out there in the market."

- Barrick Gold Q2 '25 Conference Call (emphasis added).

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

As for what the company plans to do with the proceeds of these divestments, it sounds like we could potentially see another special dividend, similar to the one in 2021 ($750 million returned through three equal tranches of $250 million or $0.14/share) related to a portion of proceeds from the sale of KCGM (50%), Lagunas Norte, and Massawa. If this is the case, the dividend could come in above US$1.00/share in 2026, with US$0.10 from the base dividend ($0.10/quarter), US$0.60 from the performance dividend (tied to net cash) of $0.15/quarter, and another US$0.10 - US$0.15 from a portion of divestment proceeds from these smaller miners.

The latter obviously depends on if/when it sells Tongon, Hemlo and Zaldivar (50%) and what price it gets for these assets, so should not be relied upon. However, the point is that there looks to be upside above what's already set to be a material increase in the dividend ($0.40/share annualized as of H1'25 with no performance dividend to $1.00/share annualized starting in 2026 with full performance dividend of $0.15/share per quarter), with this special dividend potentially being paid as early as late 2026.

Reko Diq

As for Reko Diq in Balochistan, Barrick shared that construction is ramping up on schedule for the mega-scale asset, and while there are many critics of venturing into Balochistan, it's certainly a risk I think is worth taking given the quality of this asset. In fact, Reko Diq's estimated NPV (8%) sits at ~$23 billion at $4.50/lb copper and $3,000/oz gold with 50% of this attributable to Barrick. Hence, if the company can manage this without issue, this is a game-changer for the company in terms of free cash flow generation.

It's also worth noting that Barrick is having drill success north of the area of its LOMP, with drilling 4 kilometers from the Western Porphyries suggesting the potential for another major copper-gold porphyry. In Q1-25, Barrick shared a hit of 902 meters at 0.50% copper and 0.19 G/T of gold in hole-1183, and shared that assays were pending for hole-1184 200 meters north. Hole-1184 came back with even better results (974 meters at 0.51% copper and 0.25 G/T of gold from 24 meters) and another step-out (hole-1188, drilled east of hole-1184) hit an even better intercept of 482 meters at 0.80% copper and 0.32 G/T of gold.

These are exceptional intercepts from this new prospect, Bukit Pasir, which was an undercover look-alike of H14/H15, two of the deposits at its Western Porphyries as well as H15 and H79, which are believed to be a single porphyry system.

Reserves

As for reserves, Barrick shared that it plans to replace 80% of mined depletion this year, a solid metric given that it continues to use one of the most conservative gold price assumptions sector-wide to calculate reserves at $1,400/oz. Importantly, Barrick's trend in reserves will not be harmed by the Donlin sale as it was not in reserves unlike some companies that carry low-priority (i.e. not getting built any time soon) assets in reserves to inflate their reserves). Further, this reserve growth does not include any contribution from Fourmile where I would not be surprised to see an 8+ million ounce reserve (100% basis) by the time it releases a Feasibility Study later this decade (PFS planned for 2026, not FS).

Valuation

Today, Barrick still trades at a steep discount to its closest peer, Newmont, which while larger scale, now trades at nearly double Barrick's enterprise value despite Barrick having a far superior growth profile, superior per share growth metrics and the ability to play significant catch-up from a GEO scale standpoint looking out to 2030.

Further, if Newmont (NEM) wants a piece of Fourmile, which would only be logical given the unrivaled quality of this development asset, it's going to have to pay up for it, suggesting an even wider gap of the valuations of both companies given that Newmont will likely be making a significant payment to Barrick later this decade to earn 38.5% in Fourmile. In terms of valuing the asset and Newmont's price to get a piece of Fourmile (Barrick: 100% owned), the fair market value is the cash purchase price a knowledgeable buyer would pay in an arm length transaction. And if the two parties can't agree on that fair market value, independent experts are appointed to set the fair market value.

Without knowing the gold price assumptions used to determine fair market value, what the ultimate Feasibility Study will look like and other factors when this is decided on the completion of a FS by a mutually agreed third-party engineering company, it's tough to speculate on the payment. However, it would appear that this will be a number close to $2.0 billion based on a 38.5% interest.

So, what's a fair value for Barrick?

Using what I believe to be fair multiples of 8.5x FY2026 cash flow estimates ($3,100/oz gold) and 1.1x P/NAV (7%) and conservative long-term gold price assumptions, I see a fair value for Barrick of US$29.20, translating to a 12% upside from current levels. If we switch the 2026 gold price assumption to $3,400/oz, fair value increases to closer to over US$30.00/share.

However, these figures don't do Barrick justice on a long-term basis given that gold-equivalent production is set to grow 30% between now and the end of this decade at the same time as significant growth capex rolls off.

And if we look at Barrick's potential in 2029, the company should generate over $6.0 billion in free cash flow in 2029 which at a 13x multiple (10-year average: 18x) and ~1.69 billion shares translates to a fair value of ~US$79.0 billion or ~$47.00/share. This represents nearly a 100% upside from current levels and does not require any gold price upside. So, from a long-term perspective, there is considerable upside left in the stock, and I would expect any 20% plus pullbacks to provide buying opportunities.

Technical Picture

As for the technical picture, Barrick's long-term technical chart could not be more bullish, with its monthly chart also turning the corner recently. This is because Barrick is finally back above all of its long-term moving averages which are now trending higher for the first time since early 2019. As for its yearly chart (shown below), Barrick is breaking out of the handle of a massive cup & handle base, a similar setup to the one that Agnico Eagle broke out of in Q2 '24. As we can see, AEM has outperformed the sector since, and this sharp move higher in AEM was supported by a very attractive valuation.

Today, Barrick has a very similar setup as a coiled spring with a breakout of the handle of a 15+ year cup/handle base and a valuation that's supportive of much higher prices.

That being said and while Barrick already has ultra-low expectations built in and did a better job with guidance this year being more conservative, it's hard to rule out a slight miss vs. its guidance midpoint of 3,325,000 ounces of gold this year even with a much stronger H2'25, sitting at ~1,555,000 ounces, or ~46.7% YTD. As for costs, they are also tracking to come in well above the top end of guidance ($1,460/oz - $1,560/oz), though much of this is because of higher royalties, like the rest of the peer group ($5/oz impact per $100/oz move in gold price on AISC), with its original cost guidance based on $2,400/oz.

Summary

Barrick is an unloved gold producer because of significant underperformance, with this partially being self-inflicted because of a slower and more challenging than planned ramp up at Pueblo Viejo and less than stellar delivery on guidance over the past three years.

In its defense, it has done an exceptional job replacing reserves, it has massively over-delivered on exploration, and has been very disciplined when it comes to buybacks. Adding to its issues, the sentiment and multiples tied to West African gold assets remain in the gutter and have seen compression, and it hasn't helped that Barrick has temporarily lost its best asset in an ongoing dispute in Mali, taking a bite out of one of its largest producing mines with the lowest costs (2024: ~578,000 ounces at $1,304/oz).

On a positive note, Barrick has an incredible project pipeline with several world-class assets and other high-quality mid-scale assets set to come online/ramp to full capacity later this decade (Reko Diq, Lumwana Super Pit, Goldrush, Robertson, REN). Meanwhile, it has arguably the best undeveloped asset in a Tier-1 ranked jurisdiction sector-wide in Fourmile, which can be used as a lever to materially reduce costs at its Nevada Hub by feeding much higher-grade ore to its Carlin Complex.

Further, it has a strong balance sheet with a performance dividend tied to net cash, with spot gold prices meaning that dividends will continue to climb even with Barrick remaining aggressive on buybacks and further extending its per share lead vs. peers like Newmont that have grown at the expense of heavy dilution. 2025 is shaping up to be an average year operationally, and I would be very surprised to see beats on its guidance mid-points (production/costs).

However, expectations are already at low levels, 2025 has seen an improvement from the past few years operationally, and we should finally see a trend towards improving costs starting in 2026, a welcome development given that this has been one major criticism relative to sector leaders like Agnico that have done an exceptional job keeping a lid on costs.

Today, Barrick does not screen cheaply on EV/FCF, being in a major investment phase vs. its peers. However, Barrick does screen very attractively on a P/CF and P/NAV standpoint and trades at a steep discount to peers. In addition, coming out of this growth phase, Barrick can do $6.0+ billion in free cash flow in 2029 at lower than spot gold price assumptions and that is even without Fourmile, suggesting much higher free cash flow figures in the early 2030s with Fourmile and Reko Diq Phase 2 online.

In my view, a company capable of generating $6.0 billion in free cash flow in 2029 should not be trading at a ~$45 billion market cap and while this growth is three years away, the market is likely to start re-rating the stock along the way as long as it can start delivering better on its promises and see an improvement in costs in its base business.

Given the combination of low expectations built into the stock, a world-class pipeline and significant and growing shareholder returns, I see Barrick as the best catch-up trade among the majors today (2.0+ million ounce producers), and one of the better buy-the-dip candidates within the producer space.

data.title: A Housing Bust Now Seems Inevitable
data.description:
I am circling back on a topic that should get more attention than it is currently receiving in the financial press. That is the moribund housing sector as it looks like existing home sales in the U.S. will reach their lowest levels since 1995 for the third year in a row, here in 2025.

While average home sale prices continue to inch up, it feels the market is on the verge of turning over and has in several major metros. A housing bust feels more and more inevitable at this point after the huge appreciation in home prices in the years following the pandemic. This was mostly powered by easy money policies from the Federal Reserve which boosted the money supply by roughly 40% in two years due to Covid. The central bank also bought mortgage-backed securities or MBS that helped drive 30-Year mortgages down under three percent at their trough.

That largess has ended, and mortgage rates have more than doubled since the Federal Reserve embarked on the most aggressive monetary policy since the days of Paul Volcker in March of 2022.

Rising mortgage rates, home price appreciation, and rising costs for insurance, taxes, HOAs, etc. have pushed home affordability to historical lows. The result is we are starting to see home prices head south in many regions of the country like Florida, Texas, Arizona and Nevada. The same downward pressure on home prices is likely to sweep over the rest of the U.S. in the coming years.

How bad could it get? I think we can look to Austin, Texas for clues on the potential decline. Driven by tech jobs and the explosion of the virtual workforce as well as intrastate migration from other states like California; the housing market in Austin was one of the first major markets to see housing prices go parabolic.

The Austin housing market peaked in June of 2022. Average home prices have now sunk 23% from that peak since. Why does a nationwide housing bust now feel 'in the cards'? For the simple fundamental economic concepts of Supply and Demand. Let's take a look at both below.

Supply:

Outside of a blip up during the Covid lockdowns, the supply of single home inventory reached its highest level in July since 2017. And it will get worse from here. Home builders now have more single-family homes under construction than at any time since November 2007. That was when the Housing Boom was turning into the Housing Bust that immediately preceded the Great Financial Crisis.

What is interesting or insane, depending on one's view, is this is happening even as home builders' incentive costs (free upgrades, mortgage buydowns, etc..) needed to move existing home inventory has skyrocketed. Giant home builder Lennar (LEN) has seen its incentive costs move to over 13% of sales in 2025, from under two percent in housing boom years following the pandemic. This is the highest level of incentives since 2009.

Then we have the Baby Boomers that own approximately 41% of the housing stock in the United States. Nearly 6,000 Baby Boomers are passing away daily now. The 'Silent Generation' owns nine percent of U.S. housing stock and are passing away at an even greater percentage of that population. Some of these now ownerless homes will be passed along as inheritances. However, this is a huge amount of supply that will come on the market in the coming years. How many Americans want to or can live in their parents' homes after all?

Then, we have FHA loans, that require low down payments and are usually the first to go 'underwater' whenever housing prices decline. There are some 7.8 million FHA mortgages in the U.S. market. The extreme loss mitigation efforts that were instituted during the Covid era are finally coming mostly to a close at the beginning of the federal government's fiscal year (October 1st).

It is important to note that the delinquency rate on FHA mortgages was just over 11% at the end of 2024 even with these loss mitigation efforts. How much worse will it get when these programs are curtailed? How many of these homes will go into default and get dumped on an already deteriorating market? As my late father would quip 'Only the Shadow knows'.

Demand:

And where is all the demand for all this supply supposed to come from with home affordability near historic lows? Probably not from the Millennials or Gen Z generations that account for most of the over $1.6 trillion in student loans outstanding, whose payments have recently restarted after a four-year taxpayer funded hiatus. This has already negatively affected credit scores for millions of individuals and student loan delinquency rates are soaring.

What about the potential for foreign demand picking up? This makes some sense as the dollar index has fallen just over 10% in 2025, making U.S. assets cheaper. However, global growth is tepid and major economies like Japan and Europe are struggling. Sanctions on Russia, takes potential demand from that country off the table. And is important to local markets like Miami Beach. Then, we have largest source of foreign demand for U.S. housing assets, Canada.

Let's just say our neighbor to the north is not currently enthralled with the current administration. Canada accounts for just over one third of foreign demand for U.S. homes. Canadian are increasingly selling their U.S. properties, driven by many factors including home price appreciation, rising costs of carrying homes and a weak Canadian dollar. It also should be noted that foreign demand only makes up less than 1.5% of all demand for U.S. housing.

Then we have the mass re-migration out of the U.S. happening under the new administration. Approximately 1.6 million immigrants have left the U.S. so far in 2025, mostly via self-deportation. That is around a three million run rate annually. With immigration enforcement efforts receiving a massive funding boost with the FY2026 federal budget, this is a trend that is going to continue to play out in the coming years.

What will that do to demand for rental apartments and homes? Average rental rates have already started to flatline in recent quarters (see above). It is important to note this does not capture the increasing use of incentives (Ex, two months free on a 14-month lease) rental properties are employing to fill vacancies. 28% of listings included landlord concessions in the first quarter.

Will slowing rent appreciation put pressure on investors to put more inventory on the markets? This is important as approximately 20% of the 86 million single family homes in the U.S. are owned by investors.

In summary, the market for single family homes is deteriorating rapidly. The situation for condos is much worse with average condo prices having their sharpest year-over-year drop in July in over a decade. This article focused only on the single-family home market.

So, what impacts would an average 20% home price decline across the nation, like has already happened in Austin, have on the economy and the markets. It should be noted that housing-related activities account for 15% to 18% of GDP.

In addition, what would a huge drop in average home prices have on consumer confidence? It also should be noted that U.S. consumer confidence is already near a decade low.

With U.S. consumers accounting for nearly 70% of economic activity, a further plunge in confidence would bode very ominously for the economy.

And with all the major indexes all selling at or right near all-time highs and trading at extreme levels using a variety of traditional valuation metrics (Price to sales ratio, Shiller PE, market cap to GDP ratio, etc..), what impacts would a huge housing decline have on the markets?

Unfortunately, I think there is a high probability we all are going to find out how a housing bust will play out across the economy and the markets in the quarters ahead. Caveat Emptor, my friends, Caveat Emptor.

data.title: Nutex Health's Quiet Buy-The-Dip Setup After A Blowout Quarter
data.description:
Elevator Thesis

Nutex Health (NASDAQ:NUTX) stock has been on fire this year, clocking over 192% gain since the start of the year.

A big part of its parabolic run is that its business isn’t just growing, it’s printing profits. With Nutex winning the arbitration battles, it’s been racking up healthy margins that most hospital chains can’t touch. On top of that, it’s basically rewriting the ER playbook with its micro-hospitals that deliver robust concierge-style care, which has struck a chord with its growing customer base.

Also, despite the run-up, Nutex stock is still trading attractively, at around 0.70 times forward sales; 83% lower than the sector median.

I rate Nutex as an excellent small-cap stock to pick up with explosive earnings power, with shares having pulled back into $80–$90 range.

Nutex’s Edge in Emergency Care

Nutex Health is far from being the usual hospital chain.

The healthcare disruptor builds micro-hospitals that are small, modern, and more about making care quicker and more personal.

Hence, there’s effectively no waiting hours, which also means there’s no chaos. With a few beds, in-house labs, and imaging, these places move quickly and feel like high-end clinics.

Additionally, Nutex isn’t just building a hospital but also carving out the entire ecosystem. At this point, it boasts a tight network of micro-hospitals that’s efficiently backed up by solid billing teams and back-office support.

The long-term play, though, is the move towards a tech-enabled, value-based care model. Think data-driven decisions, provider groups, and MSO services all plugged into a single, encompassing system.

Another competitive edge for Nutex is that it’s laser-focused on privately insured patients, forming more than 90% of its mix.

That means it’s getting paid top dollar in comparison to traditional hospitals, which deal with Medicare and Medicaid. It’s also why it's hitting more than 30%+ EBITDA margins, blowing past its competition in the likes of HCA Healthcare (HCA) at 20%.

Also, the facilities Nutex builds are a lot cheaper to develop and staff than massive hospitals. Nutex is keeping costs down while billing at full ER rates, producing a lean and profitable machine.

However, the biggest catalyst for Nutex has been its ability to flip the script on the 2022 No Surprises Act.

The Act was a gut-punch to many in the healthcare space, which limited out-of-network billing. Here’s the twist: starting in July last year, Nutex started hammering insurers with Independent Dispute Resolution (IDR) cases.

That turned out to be a winning strategy, with Nutex emerging victorious in 80% of them.

80% of them. More importantly, that’s been huge for its financials.

After a rough 2022, Nutex grew sales by 28% in 2023 and another 25% in the first half of 2024. It eventually swung from a massive loss in 2022 to a superb $52 million in net income for 2024.

With a sharp combo of micro-hospital model, over a 90% commercial payer mix, and effective arbitration tool use, Nutex’s playbook is working big time.

Q2 Earnings: A Blowout Quarter

Nutex Health released its Q2 results, which weren’t just good but a flat-out blowout.

Revenue jumped to a superb $244 million, up more than 221% year over year, beating Mr. Market’s $176 million estimate (a massive $67.5 million beat in a single quarter). The key driver of its top-line expansion was significantly higher revenue per visit, due to arbitration wins and healthier reimbursement rates.

Profitability expanded even quickly.

Gross profit surged to $124.9 million, resulting in a stellar 51.2% margin, compared with $22.6 million and 29.7% in the same quarter last year.

These fat margins underscore the success of Nutex’s micro-hospital model, backed by lean operations, high billing, and a top-line that’s scaling fast. Additionally, adjusted EBITDA numbers came in hot at $71.6 million, a tenfold jump from $6.8 million in Q2 2024.

Cash flow was even more impressive, with it collecting a record $175 million in cash during Q2, taking its operating cash flow to a whopping $27.1 million, up from $13.3 million a year ago.

For the first half of 2025, operating cash flow jumped to $78.1 million, compared to $16.3 million in the same period last year. That would’ve likely eased investor concerns about whether arbitration awards would translate into more cash.

Also, accounts receivable remain mostly elevated, though at $349 million due to the timing of the IDR process, but collection rates are healthy.

Operationally, Nutex continues to scale.

It’s currently running 24 micro-hospitals with plans to grow to an impressive 28 by the close of the year.

Same-facility volumes were flat to slightly positive, indicating sustainable improvements in reimbursement. Also, with $96 million in cash and just $35 million in total debt, the balance sheet looks robust overall. On top of that, Nutex also authorized a $25 million stock buyback to offset dilution linked to physician equity grants.

New 8-K Insights

Furthermore, Nutex dropped a juicy new 8-K report in August, clearing up a lot of the confusing accounting stuff, while dropping some big news for its investors.

First off, Nutex is restating past financials (FY2024 Form 10-K and the Q1-2025 Form 10-Q), but the good news is that it’s more of a technical fix.

Some stock-based compensation that was to be deployed to partner doctors was initially filed under equity. The auditors, though, flagged that it should’ve been booked as liabilities, which means there’s no real cash hit. Also, it has no impact on revenue, gross profit, or patient traffic.

However, by reclassifying these grants as liabilities, Nutex essentially avoids counting them as expenses up front, potentially beefing up the company’s long-term EPS.

Nutex’s share count, though, has jumped to over 5.56 million, which is more than 10% higher than the same time last year.

The company has issued over one million new shares to bring partner docs to seven new hospitals. For perspective, when the stock was trading near $5, stock deals involved handing out a ton of shares.

Now at $90, that same deal amounts to just a fraction. Sure, there’s been dilution, but it has fueled tremendous growth as well. Moreover, future grants will also require fewer shares.

Plus, Nutex is actively repurchasing its stock, including a $25 million buyback plan, which could retire 280,000 shares.

Nutex Faces Scrutiny from Short Sellers and Nasdaq

The bulls will have gotten a surprise following the short-seller Blue Orca Capital's scathing report in late July.
