The report panned Nutex for riding its much-talked-about comeback on aggressive accounting measures and a “refined timing” trick for arbitration claims.

At the heart of the allegation is the HaloMD situation, where a third-party IDR vendor is up against RICO-style lawsuits from big-ticket insurers like BCBS Georgia and Elevance. Nutex isn’t named in the suits, but clearly, the implication here is guilt by association.

Naturally, Nutex responded in quick fashion, calling the claims "misleading" and "believes the report contains misrepresentations and conveys a misunderstanding of Nutex Health's business," while defending its standardized and robust billing practices.

Also, with $175 million in Q2 cash collections and $311 million in just the first half of 2025, there is evidence that the IDR awards are being effectively monetized.

Still, risks are real. Receivables stood at $349 million, which shows some lag in cash conversion. Also, it missed its Q2 10-Q deadline, which led to a Nasdaq delinquency notice. Nutex has time until Feb. 10, 2026, to regain compliance, but the filing delay does pose some questions over its internal controls.

Nevertheless, it isn’t an automatic delisting, and the path for the bulls is clear in that Nutex needs to file cleanly, and upcoming quarterlies will throw greater weight around the veracity of its arbitration engine.

Discussing Nutex Stock’s Valuation

After a meteoric run, Nutex's stock trades more than 20× higher than a year ago. Yet even at this level, the valuation looks mostly digestible given its fundamentals and profitability.

From January to May, Nutex ripped 320%+ as it scaled IDR arbitration, efficiently juicing out-of-network pay. April’s Q4 earnings print confirmed that the top-line surge was real, then the following two quarters took things up a couple of notches.

As I mentioned earlier, Nutex is trading comfortably under 1-times its forward sales estimates.

For context, HCA Healthcare, the largest hospital chain operating in the U.S., trades closer to 1.2× sales, while Tenet Healthcare (THC) sits around 0.5×. Despite Nutex’s more impressive top-line expansion numbers and higher margins, its valuation multiple lies in the same ballpark as its slower-growing peers.

Looking at enterprise value, the gap grows even wider. Nutex posted a superb $144 million in adjusted EBITDA in the first half of 2025, which points to a $300 million run-rate for the full year.

Hence, with an enterprise value of $440 million (market cap minus net cash), Nutex trades at around 1.5× EV/EBITDA. In comparison, large hospital operators trade closer to the 8 to 10 times EV/EBITDA.

Naturally, volatility is a big part of the story.

Shares have fallen between the $60 and $100 range in recent months, but those $80–$90 pullbacks represent attractive entry points.

The power-packed combo of high growth, leaner operations, and a small-cap profile creates room for outsized moves if Nutex continues executing well and earnings remain strong.

Risks To The Thesis

Despite the more compelling bullish thesis, Nutex has multiple risks worth watching.

First up, there are regulatory risks for it to contend with, as its healthy earnings of late have been bumped by the NSA arbitration process (IDR). Any changes on that front could significantly impact its financial results.

Moreover, regulators are still refining the system, but for now, the providers are emerging as winners in the bulk of the cases. That’s a great sign, though Nutex must stay proactive as insurers potentially push back.

Growth sustainability is another big factor to consider.

The massive 200%+ top-line jump last year was mostly driven by a one-time mix of claim backlogs and favorable collections, so it's natural to expect growth to moderate from here. Also, with it up against tough comps in the upcoming quarters, its stock may not experience the same bumps it once did.

Finally, the physician partnership model involves share dilution, but the management has proactively offset that with buybacks. Hence, the overall outlook remains mostly impressive, but ongoing execution and policy awareness are critical.

Takeaway On Nutex Stock

Nutex Health has efficiently flipped ER care on its head, with its lean, profitable, and high-margin business model built around micro-hospitals. Also, with it prevailing in 80%+ of arbitration battles, disputed claims are quickly turning into real cash, fueling 30%+ EBITDA margins. Also, despite an incredible rally over the last year, Nutex stock still trades attractively across key pricing metrics, and with buybacks in play and pullbacks into the $80–$90s, it’s an excellent buy-the-dip opportunity.

data.title: Carillon Chartwell Mid Cap Value Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

The second quarter was one of the most tumultuous for U.S. equity investors in recent memory. There were several geopolitical developments with macroeconomic implications for them to consider. These included efforts by the U.S. Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) to reduce government spending, the deportation of illegal immigrants, the proposed “One Big Beautiful Bill,” a war between Israel and Iran, and the U.S. bombing of Iranian nuclear facilities, to name just a few.

But it was President Trump’s decision, announced on April 2, to impose a 10% baseline tariff on all countries and reciprocal rates as high as 50% on 60 trading partners that caused the most angst. While many investors recognized the Trump administration’s desire to rebalance global trade and its willingness to use tariffs to gain negotiating leverage with key trade partners, few anticipated tariffs of this scope and scale.

Fear of a potential trade war that would disrupt the global economy and impair corporate earnings quickly permeated the equity market. Stock prices plummeted over the next several days, extending declines that had begun months earlier into bear market territory. The panic soon spread to the U.S. Treasury and corporate credit markets, alarming President Trump and his economic advisors. On April 9, presumably in an effort to avert global recession and financial contagion, he announced a 90-day pause on all reciprocal tariffs.

Stock prices rallied sharply after President Trump’s pivot and continued to rebound through the end of the quarter as his administration pursued bilateral trade agreements with China and other countries. Astonishingly, major stock market averages ended the quarter ahead of where they started, completing a roller-coaster ride that investors will not soon forget. After declining 12% in early April, the Russell Midcap® Value Index rebounded more than 20% and generated a total return of approximately 5% in the quarter.

The company announced the sale of its poorly performing Family Dollar subsidiary and reported better than expected same-store sales growth, prompting management to raise its full-year outlook.

nVent (NVT) provides electrical connection and protection solutions, including enclosures, electric heat-tracing systems, and fastening products that support critical infrastructure across industrial, commercial, and energy markets. Investors reacted favorably to a better growth outlook, driven by growing demand from data center and utility customers.

Gates Industrial, a manufacturer of power transmission and fluid power products, benefitted from a return to growth after a prolonged period of inventory digestion.

Regal Rexnord (RRX), a manufacturer of motion control components, power transmission solutions, and specialty electric motors and controls, advanced after reporting improving order trends and issuing a better than expected outlook.

L3Harris Technologies provides advanced aerospace and defense solutions across land, air, sea, space, and cyber domains. Its shares were boosted as investors considered the positive impact on demand of increased defense spending in response to geopolitical conflicts and shifting alliances.

Bottom securities

Global Payments (GPN) provides card and digital payments technology and business operations enterprise software. The stock came under pressure after the company announced a large strategic acquisition that some investors worry will increase business complexity and create integration risk.

Avantor provides chemicals, reagents, and laboratory supplies to customers in the biopharmaceutical, healthcare, and government sectors. Share price weakness reflected investors’ concerns about government funding cuts and the cost impact of tariffs.

Baker Hughes (BKR), an energy services and industrial technology supplier, reported solid results in its growing Industrial Technology segment, but shares were pressured along with industry peers due to lower oil prices.

Globus Medical (GMED) is a medical device company selling implantable devices, accessories, and surgical instruments used in a range of spinal and orthopedic procedures. Investors were disappointed by weak results in the company’s orthopedic robotics segment.

Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA) is a real estate investment trust (REIT) focused on multi-family properties in the U.S. Sun Belt.

Outlook

Typically, bear markets create potential buying opportunities for disciplined investors, and the market meltdown in early April was no different. We were more active than usual during this period, taking advantage of panicked selling to invest in what we saw as quality companies with promising fundamental outlooks at historically low valuations. Many of these investments outperformed our expectations during the market’s rebound.

But market leadership during this period was narrow, and we believe many companies in the Carillon Chartwell Mid Cap Value Fund’s portfolio remain undervalued. Definitions

The U.S. Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE), previously the U.S. Digital Service, was created with a stated goal of maximizing governmental efficiency and productivity.

Inventory digestion refers to a company selling or otherwise disposing of an excess in inventory.

Integration risk is the possibility that merging two companies might not be produce the desired cost savings, synergies, economies of scale, market advantages, or other benefits sought in the merger.

Macroeconomics focuses on the interactions between the markets, businesses, governments, and consumers that make up an entire economy.

Market capitalization refers to the total market value of a company’s outstanding shares of stock.

Narrow refers to the relationship between the median and the mean of a market index: When a few data outliers result in a mean that is substantially larger or smaller than the median of the full data set, then the performance of the entire index is being driven by a “narrow” selection of companies.

The One Big Beautiful Bill Act made permanent tax cuts created by the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017, raised the U.S. debt ceiling by $5 trillion, and changed a range of laws on tax policy, healthcare, clean energy production, and immigration.

Overweight describes a portfolio position in an industry sector or some other category that is greater than the corresponding weight level in a benchmark portfolio.

Positioning refers to assessments of whether professional investors are, on the whole, bullish or bearish on a particular security, industry, sector, market capitalization or other area of the market, as reflected by the extent to which they are invested in the area of the market in question.

A reciprocal tariff is a tax or trade restriction that one nation imposes on another on a tit-for-tat basis in response to a trade-restriction actions taken by the nation that is the subject of the reciprocal tariff.

Total return, when measuring performance, is the actual rate of return of an investment or a pool of investments over a given period of time. Total return includes interest, capital gains, dividends, and distributions realized over the specified period.

Underweight describes a portfolio position in an industry sector or some other category that is less than the corresponding weight level in a benchmark portfolio.

Value investing is an investment strategy that involves picking stocks that appear to be trading for less than their intrinsic or book value.

Benchmark Index

The Russell Midcap® Value Index, the Fund’s benchmark index, measures the performance of those Russell Midcap companies with lower price/ book ratios and lower forecasted growth values.

The stocks are also members of the Russell 1000® Value Index.

data.title: Smith-Midland Rental Business Is Impressive, And Price Is Fair Even If Not Opportunistic
data.description:
Smith-Midland (NASDAQ:SMID) presented 2Q25 results recently.

The results were very good, with growing revenues, basically entirely driven by the rental of highway barriers. This business also shows significantly higher gross margins than that business, and impressive operating cost leverage, with flat OpEx.

I had written about the name in March 2025, with doubts about the business results' sustainability. I believe there's more evidence of that now. SMID trades at about 14x earnings if we assume 2H25 is equally good as 1H25. I'm not super sure about it. That is, the name's earnings multiple is high, but I believe this is fair. Still, not particularly opportunistic, so I maintain a Hold.

2Q25 results

Bareer rental driver: The company's business in FY24 and in 1H25 has been almost entirely driven by the new bareer rental business. In this system, instead of selling the concrete barriers used in highways, the company leases them.

This business is more tied to road repair activity, because otherwise the government or highway operator would simply buy the barrier.

In FY24, 75% of the company's revenue growth ($12 million out of $19 milion) came from barrier rental growth, which includes shipping and installation revenue. In 1H25, the entirety of the growth ($12.5 million) was driven by barrier rental and installation services.

Further, among the product categories, the big winner in both FY24 and 1H25 has been the SoundWall product, which is installed by the side of highways mostly, to avoid contaminating adjacent properties with vehicle sound.

Much higher gross margins: In FY24, the cost of sales decreased by 8pp to 78%, and so far in 1H25, another 6pp to 72%. The driver is the low value of depreciation and amortization of the barriers versus the growth in revenues.

One concern one might have is the understating of the cost of that depreciation. The company does not disclose its PP&E in a lot of detail, but 'Rental Equipment' had $12 million in gross PP&E recorded in FY24. Compared to this, the company generated $12 million in revenues from barrier rental alone (not considering installation services), and another $14 million in 1H25.

This is clearly a high-return business. The problem is durability (more on this later).

Very good OpEx leverage: Finally, the company controlled its expenses very well. In FY24, OpEx were only 6% higher versus gross profits 100% higher, and in 1H25, OpEx were flat with gross profits growing 63% YOY.

OpEx leverage during growth periods is the holy grail of business, expanding operating margins during positive periods, and potentially protecting during negative periods (as expenses have remained contained, which is easier to correct than when OpEx grows and then has to be curtailed).

Durability of the barrier rental business

This is the key question for valuing Smith Midland going forward. There are two factors to consider: demand and supply.

Demand is almost entirely driven by government spending on road repairs, in a specific zone of the country (the East Coast, see Presentation) where SMID operates. A big driver has been the IIJA Act from 2021, allocating $350 billion to road infrastructure works. The American Society of Civil Engineers believes that American roads deserve a D+ grade, one of the worst for the different infrastructure categories. Further, they believe at least $1.5 trillion in road investment has already been funded by 2033. That is, I don't think this will be a problem.

The other side, supply, is different. If the business is so profitable as SMID is showing, then competition will eventually increase. It is simply a rule of the market that when a business is profitable and has low barriers to entry (no pun intended), then capital will flow into it and margins will decrease. The question is when, and for now, I cannot answer that.

Price is fairish, not attractive

The company said in its release for 2Q25 that it doesn't expect 2H25 to be as good as 1H25: 'Service revenue for the first half of 2025 included two special barrier rental projects that are not anticipated to recur in the second half of the year.' That means operating profitability will probably be lower in 2H25 as well.

However, the company is also doubling its stock of barriers to almost 120 miles (550 thousand linear feet, according to the presentation). This means the company believes 2026/27 to be even better.

If we were to consider 2H25 being as good as 1H25 (we already know it will probably be worse because management said so), then the company would make $20 million in operating income and about $15 million after taxes.

Compared to this, the company trades at a market cap of $220 million (5.3 million shares at $41.5 per share). That is a multiple of 14.5x, approximately, and that is considering that 2H25 profitability is very good, so the NTM multiple is actually higher potentially.

At the same time, this is a company with a business growing at triple digits (4x in rental barriers 2Q25 vs 2Q24, 7x in 1H25), which is expanding gross margins and leveraging operating income.

Still, assuming no further gross margin leverage (28%) and flat OpEx ($10 million a year), the company could generate an earnings yield of 10% ($21 million in net income) only at $135 million in revenues, or 50% above where it sits today. Hard, but not impossible, considering the company has doubled revenues since FY22.

I believe the price is fair, even if a little frothy. Of course, considering the risk of competition and the fact that a lot of optimism is already built in, I don't think this is an opportunity. For that reason, I maintain a Hold, but I understand if some readers have a more bullish outlook.

data.title: YMAX Vs. YMAG: Both Offer High Yield Income Weekly, Both Are Very High-Risk Investments
data.description:
As a retired passive income investor, I enjoy researching and writing about high yield investments that offer a regular stream of passive income deposited into my retirement account every month, or in some cases, every week. I understand the risks involved with some of these funds that I like to write about, but I may not always focus on those risks when I do write about an investment that I own. As the stock market continues to make new highs and the risk of a market correction draws nearer with each passing day, I feel that any current recommendation that I offer should probably come with a healthy dose of warning regarding the potential risks.

A little bit more about my style of investing is probably a necessary prerequisite for an article like this one. I call my approach my Income Compounder strategy and portfolio. The funds that I like to own include CEFs (closed-end funds) and ETFs (exchange-traded funds) that offer very high yield income that I compound monthly, and now weekly in the case of several funds that I own. As a retiree, my investment portfolio is used to supplement my retirement income (from Social Security and a pension) but is not the sole source of my net worth. Therefore, I can afford to take on more risk with my investments than some other retired investors who may not feel comfortable taking similar chances.

I am also a net buyer of income funds, mostly reinvest the distributions, and I rarely sell any shares unless it is to capture capital gains from price appreciation, which I then use to reinvest into more income-producing shares of whatever holding I own that is on sale at the time.

In this review, I would like to offer my analysis and opinion of two very high-risk/high-reward ETFs from the YieldMax family of funds. One of those is the YieldMax Magnificent 7 Fund of Option Income ETFs (NYSEARCA:YMAG), which I first reviewed back in February of this year when I rated it a Buy. Since that analysis was published, the fund has lost -10% in price but delivered a total return of 13%.

The other fund that I would like to compare and contrast with YMAG is the YieldMax Universe Fund of Option Income ETFs (YMAX). The approach used by YMAX is similar to that used by YMAG with a fund of funds strategy where most of the fund holdings are other YieldMax ETFs. The performance over the past year has been quite similar and both funds pay weekly distributions.

In fact, both YMAG and YMAX delivered a total return exceeding 25% over the past year, outperforming the S&P 500 (SP500).

However, the fund holdings in YMAX are more diverse. Whereas YMAG holds the 7 YieldMax ETFs that are single asset covered call funds for each of the Mag 7 stocks, YMAX holds many more funds in a variety of different asset classes, with the top 10 holdings accounting for only about 40% of the total.

Underlying fund assets held in YMAX in addition to traditional equities include securities based on gold, energy, bitcoin, and other alternative investments. According to the YieldMax website, YMAX is a true fund of funds that gets rebalanced monthly.

The Fund is a “fund of funds,” meaning that it will primarily invest its assets in certain YieldMax™ ETFs. The Fund’s portfolio will be reallocated on a monthly basis so that each YieldMax™ ETF held in the Fund’s portfolio, including any eligible new YieldMax™ ETF that is added to the portfolio, is equally weighted.

The risks to investing in YMAX are also clearly indicated on the website and are even highlighted in bold print.

Investing in the fund involves a high degree of risk. Due to the investment strategies of the Underlying YieldMax™ ETFs, the Fund’s indirect exposure to gains, if any, of the share price returns of the Underlying Securities is capped. However, the Fund is subject to all potential losses if the shares of the Underlying Securities decrease in value, which may not be offset by income received by the Fund.

Note that the fund’s primary objective is to seek current income. This is not the same as “seeking alpha,” which implies seeking a higher total return than the S&P 500. Even though the two funds have delivered alpha relative to the SPY over the past year, that is not the primary reason to invest in these funds.

YMAX and YMAG Distribution History

YMAX

Both YMAG and YMAX pay a variable distribution that is based on the options premiums generated by the underlying fund holdings. In the case of YMAX, which first started paying monthly distributions in March 2024 then switched to weekly starting in September 2024, the distribution history has been variable yet fairly steady within a tight range over the past year as shown on the SA Dividend History page.

According to the YieldMax website, the current distribution rate is 44.6% based on the latest distribution if it remained the same going forward, annualized.

Also important to note, for investors who hold YMAX in a taxable account, there is a large amount of ROC (return of capital) included in the distributions as indicated on the screenshot shown above. That can be an advantage for investors who wish to benefit from the reduction of cost basis associated with ROC, but can also be a deterrent for those investors who may be concerned that a large amount of ROC could have a negative impact on the NAV of the fund.

YMAG

On the other hand, YMAG offers a similar distribution rate (albeit slightly higher at a current rate of 55%) with no ROC indicated in the most recent distribution.

The distribution history for YMAG is also similar to YMAX with monthly distributions initially, changing to weekly in September 2024. There is a bit more variability in the YMAG distributions compared to YMAX.

Past Performance and Expected Future Performance

Both funds are relatively new with both YMAG and YMAX going public in January 2024. As mentioned above, the performance of both funds has been nearly identical since inception.

The performance of each underlying fund is crucial to the overall fund performance so let’s take a brief look at each of the fund’s top holdings individual performance.

YMAG Holdings

According to SA as of 8/21/15, the top holdings in YMAG are shown in the screenshot below.

GOOY

Although GOOY declined by more than -16% in market price over the last year, total return performance was about 10% according to the fund website.

APLY

APLY lost more than -25% in market price over the last year, however, it yields a cool 36 to 40% distribution rate. Performance over the past year has not been great but the fund appears to be stabilizing and will likely continue to generate high yield income in the coming months.

NVDY

This is my personal favorite of the YieldMax funds, and I own shares in NVDY as well as in YMAG.

Although NVDY lost nearly a third of its market value based on price over the last year, the extremely high yield income more than compensated for it, delivering a total return of about 33% over a one-year period.

AMZY

AMZY is another YieldMax ETF that I like a lot and also own shares of.

Although AMZY lost about -20% in price over the last year, the yield of more than 50% compensated for it quite handily, delivering one-year performance of about 23%.

TSLY

TSLY lost more than -40% in share price over the last year, however, the price appears to be stabilizing at around $8 and offers a compelling yield. One year performance is not bad at around 13%, and the three-month performance of about 9% bodes well for improved future performance.

FBY

FBY appears to be stabilizing at a price of around $16 and offers a reasonably high yield income stream as well. FBY has been one of the better performing of the YMAG holdings with a 50% total return over the past year.

MSFO

While MSFO has lost only about -12% in price over the past year, the lowest yielding of the YMAG funds has delivered a reasonable total return at about 24% of NAV.

YMAX Fund Holdings – Brief Review

Alas, I cannot take up the page space with a similar analysis of each of the YMAX fund holdings because there are far too many of them. You can download a spreadsheet with the complete list of fund holdings if you are interested. Here is a list of the tickers that make up the primary fund holdings in YMAX, bold tickers are the 7 YMAG funds:

AMDY

AMZY

APLY

BABO

BRKC

CONY

CVNY

DISO

FBY

FGXXX (Treasuries)

GDXY

GOOY

HOOY

JPMO

MARO

MSFO

MSTY

NFLY

NVDY

OARK

PLTY

PYPY

SMCY

SNOY

TSLY

TSMY

XOMO

XYZY

YBIT

There are also some additional short positions in individual stocks and some cash and other investments included in the complete list of YMAX fund holdings. I have not done an in-depth review of the YMAX fund and leave that exercise up to readers who may be interested.

Summary: I prefer YMAG to YMAX

For my purposes, and based on my own investing goals, risk tolerance, and expectations of future performance, I like the YMAG fund and own some shares in my own Income Compounder portfolio. However, I do not own more than about 3% of my total amount invested and I take the weekly distributions and use them to reinvest in whatever holding that I own and that is “on sale” at the time. It may mean buying more shares of YMAG, or it could be one of about 40 other income holdings that I own shares of.

I can also see the value of holding YMAX instead since it includes the 7 YMAG funds in its holdings, and the additional holdings may provide more diversification to reduce overall risk. There is an argument to be made either way. This review was intended to alert readers to the idea that a solid income stream that is paid weekly is available from these two YieldMax funds and it is up to the reader to decide whether it makes sense to add some shares to their own income-oriented portfolios.
